disease.name: Acute Cholecystitis
disease.note: Calculous cholecystitis from cystic duct obstruction by a gallstone. Classic presentation: RUQ pain after fatty meal, associated with nausea, fever. Murphy's sign is pathognomonic. In East Africa, gallstone disease is increasing with westernization of diet, but acalculous cholecystitis is also common in ICU patients with typhoid, malaria, or sepsis. In patients with sickle cell disease (common in Kenya), pigment stones cause cholecystitis at a younger age.
presentingComplaints: Pain under the ribs on the right side
Pain that spreads to the right shoulder or back
Pain after eating fatty or fried food

history_questions.question: Where is the pain exactly — in the right upper part of your abdomen under your ribs?
Does the pain radiate to your right shoulder or to your back between your shoulder blades?
Did the pain start after eating a fatty or fried meal (e.g., fried fish, chapati, samosa, pilau, mandazi)?
Have you had similar attacks before that resolved on their own?
Do you have fever or chills?
Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes or your urine becoming dark like tea?
Have you noticed your stools becoming pale or clay-coloured?
Have you vomited? Is the vomit yellow/green (bilious)?
Do you have a known history of gallstones? Have you had an ultrasound before?
Do you have sickle cell disease or trait? (Common cause of pigment stones in our population)
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries?
Are you diabetic? Do you have high blood pressure or HIV?
Have you had episodes of jaundice in the past?
Do you have any known liver disease or hepatitis?
Are you on any medications (especially birth control pills or hormone therapy)?
Have you lost weight recently? Very rapid weight loss can cause gallstones.
For women: How many pregnancies have you had? Are you pregnant now?
Do you feel pain when pressing on your right upper abdomen? Does a deep breath make it worse?
Have you been admitted to the ICU recently or had a major illness? (For acalculous cholecystitis risk)
Does the pain wake you up at night or does it interfere with your sleep?
Have you had any episodes of fainting, dizziness, or near-collapse with the pain?
Do you feel bloated or excessively gassy even when you have not eaten much?
Have you experienced heartburn or acid reflux that is not relieved by your usual medication?
Did you have any recent blood transfusion? (linked to hemolysis and pigment stones)
Are you of indigenous Kenyan or East African descent? Do you have a family history of gallstones?
Have you been diagnosed with chronic liver disease or cirrhosis in the past?
Do you drink alcohol regularly? If yes, how much and how often?
Have you had any previous reactions to antibiotics or anaesthesia?
For men: Do you have any difficulty or pain with urination? (prostate issues can present with vague abdominal pain)
Have you had any snake bite or scorpion sting recently? (can cause hemolysis and pigment stones)
Do you feel the pain is worse when you breathe in deeply or when you press on your ribs?
Have you noticed your abdomen swelling up or feeling hard to the touch?
Did you take any traditional herbal remedies or visit a traditional healer before coming?
Have you had any episodes of confusion, especially if you are elderly? (may indicate severe sepsis)
Have you travelled to a malaria-endemic region recently? Malaria can cause hemolysis and acalculous cholecystitis.
Do you have a history of HIV? If yes, what is your CD4 count and are you on ARVs?
Have you recently been treated for typhoid fever? Salmonella can cause acalculous cholecystitis.
Do you feel generally unwell, body-ached, or have joint pains before the abdominal pain started?
Is there any history of gallbladder or biliary tract cancer in your family?

historyFeatures.note: Suggests CBD obstruction or Mirizzi syndrome

examination_findings.finding: Murphy's sign positive (arrest of inspiration on deep palpation of RUQ)
Right upper quadrant tenderness
Guarding in right upper quadrant
Rebound tenderness in RUQ (suggests gangrene/perforation)
Palpable gallbladder mass (Courvoisier's sign if associated with cancer)
Jaundice (icterus)
Fever (temperature >38°C)
Tachycardia
Boas' sign (right infrascapular hyperesthesia)
Abdominal distension (if ileus from inflammation)
Generalized peritonitis (if perforation)
Right shoulder tip pain on deep palpation (phrenic nerve irritation)
Scratch marks on skin (if chronic pruritus from obstructive jaundice)
Dehydration signs
Murphy's sign absent — may be negative in gangrenous/emphysematous cholecystitis
Tender hepatomegaly on deep palpation
Guarding in both RUQ and epigastrium (suggests concomitant pancreatitis)
Right flank dullness to percussion (if subphrenic abscess or hepatomegaly)
Hypoactive bowel sounds (paralytic ileus from adjacent inflammation)
Positive psoas sign on the right (if gallbladder inflammation irritates psoas)
Positive obturator sign (if inflammation extends to pelvis)
Chest auscultation: reduced breath sounds at right base (sympathetic pleural effusion — "sympathetic right pleural effusion of cholecystitis")
Right costovertebral angle tenderness (may mimic pyelonephritis)
Pallor (if chronic disease or hemolysis from sickle cell)
Palpable lymphadenopathy — right supraclavicular (Virchow's node if gallbladder cancer)
Cachexia or muscle wasting (suggests underlying malignancy rather than acute cholecystitis)
Pleural effusion on auscultation — right base (sympathetic effusion in severe disease)
Petechiae or bruising (coagulopathy from obstructive jaundice or liver disease)
Ascites (if underlying cirrhosis or portal hypertension — common in our setting)
Peripheral oedema (suggests liver disease or malnutrition — poor surgical candidate)
Fetor hepaticus (musty breath odour — if underlying liver failure)
Asterixis (liver flap — if hepatic encephalopathy from acute-on-chronic liver disease)

components.item: Grade I (Mild): Mild inflammation, no organ dysfunction, mild local changes
Grade II (Moderate): WBC >18k, palpable mass, duration >72h, local complications (empyema, abscess, gangrene)
Grade III (Severe): Organ dysfunction (CV, respiratory, renal, hepatic, neurological)

interpretation.low: Grade I: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72h. IV antibiotics.
interpretation.moderate: Grade II: Urgent cholecystectomy. Higher conversion rate. Consider percutaneous drainage if high risk.
interpretation.high: Grade III: ICU admission. Organ support. Percutaneous cholecystostomy. Delayed cholecystectomy after recovery.

interpretation.low: Score 0-2: Mild disease. Check for CBD stones. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
interpretation.moderate: Score 3-4: Moderate to severe. High suspicion of complicated disease. ICU monitoring recommended.
interpretation.high: Score 5-7: Critical. Likely CBD obstruction, pancreatitis, or sepsis. Urgent ERCP/MRCP ± drainage.

riskFactors.note: Most episodes triggered by stone impaction in cystic duct
Biliary stasis during pregnancy. Common in Kenyan women with many children.
Pigment stone formation from chronic hemolysis — presents younger
Higher risk of emphysematous cholecystitis, higher morbidity
Risk factor for acalculous cholecystitis — hypotension, NPO, positive pressure ventilation
Salmonella typhi can cause acalculous cholecystitis; chronic carrier state

subtypes.name: Calculous Cholecystitis (Stone-Related)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of onset (early cholecystectomy). IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone 2 g daily + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly). Single-stage procedure if no CBD stones. If CBD stones present: preoperative ERCP or laparoscopic CBD exploration.

subtypes.name: Acalculous Cholecystitis
subtypes.management: Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negative and anaerobes (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Meropenem). Percutaneous cholecystostomy (US- or CT-guided) as definitive treatment in high-risk ICU patients. Interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy after recovery if patient becomes surgical candidate.

subtypes.name: Emphysematous Cholecystitis
subtypes.management: Emergency cholecystectomy (open or laparoscopic). High rate of conversion. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Carbapenem + Metronidazole). ICU admission. Clostridium perfringens and E. coli are common organisms. Mortality up to 15-25%.

subtypes.name: Gangrenous Cholecystitis
subtypes.management: Emergency laparoscopic or open cholecystectomy. High conversion rate due to inflammation and necrosis. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. ICU monitoring post-operatively. High risk of bile duct injury.

subtypes.name: Mirizzi Syndrome (Type I-IV)
subtypes.management: Type I: Subtotal cholecystectomy with careful dissection of the cystic duct — leaving a cuff of gallbladder infundibulum to avoid bile duct injury. Types II-IV: Requires repair of the fistula — either primary closure over a T-tube (Type II), hepaticojejunostomy Roux-en-Y (Types III-IV). High risk of bile duct injury. Preoperative MRCP/ERCP essential for planning. Referral to a hepatobiliary surgeon strongly recommended. In our setting where advanced hepatobiliary expertise is scarce, a subtotal cholecystectomy with stone extraction and fistula closure over a drain may be the safest option.

subtypes.name: Hydrops of the Gallbladder (Mucocele)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic or open cholecystectomy. The gallbladder is often very large — care with extraction to avoid rupture. Consider aspiration of contents to facilitate removal. Interval cholecystectomy if patient is high risk and the hydrops is sterile — but risk of subsequent empyema or perforation is significant.

mimics.whyMimic: Epigastric/RUQ pain, guarding, peritonism can mimic cholecystitis
Epigastric/RUQ pain, vomiting, associated with gallstones can mimic cholecystitis
RUQ pain, fever, guarding can mimic cholecystitis especially in children
RUQ pain, jaundice, nausea, anorexia, fever in early phase
Can present with right flank pain radiating to groin, sometimes confused with biliary pain
RUQ pain, fever, tenderness, leukocytosis — especially amoebic or pyogenic liver abscess common in East Africa

mimics.diseaseName: Biliary Dyskinesia / Sphincter of Oddi Dysfunction
mimics.whyMimic: Recurrent RUQ/epigastric pain after meals, nausea, bloating — identical presentation to biliary colic or mild cholecystitis
RUQ pain, fever, tenderness — chlamydial or gonococcal perihepatitis mimics cholecystitis in young sexually active women
Epigastric/RUQ discomfort, nausea, diaphoresis — atypical presentation of inferior MI can simulate cholecystitis, especially in elderly diabetics
Epigastric/RUQ pain, nausea, vomiting, anorexia — infectious gastroenteritis can mimic early cholecystitis
Dull RUQ pain, change in bowel habits, anemia — colonic tumor at the hepatic flexure can present with RUQ discomfort and mimic cholecystitis
Right flank/RUQ pain, fever, leukocytosis — renal or perinephric abscess can present with RUQ pain and fever identical to cholecystitis

bedside.name: Murphy's sign testing
bedside.indication: All patients with RUQ pain — pathognomonic when positive

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: RUQ/epigastric pain can be referred from inferior MI — mandatory in elderly and diabetics

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis >10,000 supports acute infection. >18,000 suggests TG Grade II severity.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: CRP >10 mg/L supports inflammation. >200 mg/L suggests gangrene or severe disease.

laboratory.name: Liver Function Tests (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, Bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: Obstructive pattern: elevated ALP, GGT, bilirubin suggests CBD stones. AST/ALT elevation suggests hepatitis or CBD obstruction.

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase / Lipase
laboratory.indication: Rule out acute pancreatitis — gallstone pancreatitis may coexist.

laboratory.name: Serum Albumin
laboratory.indication: Low albumin <32 g/L suggests malnutrition and worse prognosis (Glasgow score criterion)

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: Assess dehydration and renal function — pre-operative baseline.

laboratory.name: Blood cultures
laboratory.indication: Positive in 20-30% of acute cholecystitis. E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus common.

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile
laboratory.indication: Cholestasis → impaired vitamin K absorption → prolonged PT/INR.

laboratory.name: Blood Glucose
laboratory.indication: Diabetic patients have higher morbidity. Stress hyperglycemia.

laboratory.name: Blood Group and Crossmatch
laboratory.indication: Pre-operative preparation — especially if redo surgery or coagulopathy.

imaging.name: Right Upper Quadrant Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line imaging. Shows: gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign, CBD dilatation >7mm. Sensitivity 85-90%.

imaging.name: HIDA Scan (Cholescintigraphy)
imaging.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis when ultrasound equivocal. Non-visualization of gallbladder 60 min after tracer injection = cystic duct obstruction. Sensitivity 95%.

imaging.name: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: Non-invasive evaluation of CBD stones. Sensitivity >90% for CBD stones. Used when ERCP is planned for stone extraction.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Used when complications suspected: gangrene (gas in wall, perfusion defects), emphysematous cholecystitis, perforation, abscess. Better for extra-biliary pathology.

imaging.name: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: Therapeutic — for CBD stone extraction. Sphincterotomy + stone removal. Pre-operative if high probability of CBD stones (jaundice, dilated CBD, stones on US). Combined with laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: Exclude right lower lobe pneumonia as mimic. Free air under diaphragm suggests perforated ulcer not cholecystitis.

management.protocolReference: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) for the Management of Acute Cholecystitis

resuscitation: NBM (nil by mouth) — prepare for possible surgery
IV fluids — Normal Saline or Ringer's Lactate 1-2 L bolus if dehydrated, then 125 mL/hr
IV antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + Metronidazole 500 mg IV 8-hourly (first-line)
Alternatively Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g IV 8-hourly for severe cases
Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly; IV Morphine 2.5-5 mg PRN for breakthrough pain
NG tube if vomiting or distension
Urinary catheter if septic or elderly requiring fluid monitoring
Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics if febrile >38°C
conservative: Monitor vitals 4-hourly: BP, HR, temperature, SpO2
Daily LFT and WBC/CRP trend monitoring
Ultrasound to confirm diagnosis and assess for complications

surgical: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy — gold standard (early, within 72h of symptom onset)
Early cholecystectomy reduces total hospital stay, cost, and complications vs delayed
Open cholecystectomy if laparoscopic not feasible (severe inflammation, Mirizzi syndrome, suspected cancer, previous multiple upper abdominal surgeries)
Intraoperative cholangiogram if CBD anatomy unclear or suspected CBD stones
Subtotal cholecystectomy (laparoscopic or open) if Calot's triangle is hostile — leave posterior wall, close stump — safer than bile duct injury
Conversion from laparoscopic to open: 5-15% (severe inflammation, bleeding, unclear anatomy)
nonOperative: Percutaneous cholecystostomy (US/CT-guided drain placement) for: Grade III severe cholecystitis in unstable ICU patients, very high surgical risk (ASA 4), acalculous cholecystitis
ERCP + sphincterotomy if concurrent CBD stones with cholangitis — for stone retrieval, followed by delayed cholecystectomy
Interval cholecystectomy 6-8 weeks after resolution of severe episode (if initial non-operative management)

immediate: IV fluids until tolerating oral intake
IV antibiotics: continue for 24h post-op (non-complicated) or 5-7 days (gangrenous, empyema, perforation)
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily starting 6h post-op
NG tube removal at end of surgery or day 1 if output <500 mL
Pain control: IV Paracetamol + NSAIDs ± Morphine PRN. Transition to oral as tolerated.
Monitor drain output if drain placed (bilious suggests bile leak)
day1_3: Advance diet: sips → clear fluids → soft diet as tolerated
Remove drain if output <50 mL/day and non-bilious
Check Hb, WBC, LFTs, amylase post-op day 1
Plan discharge for uncomplicated cases: day 1-2
Review histology of gallbladder at follow-up
day3_7: Continue IV antibiotics if complicated case — complete 5-7 day course
If drain still in situ (complicated case): monitor output, amylase content
Wound review — suture removal day 7-10 outpatient

pathways.criteria: TG18 Grade I: Mild cholecystitis, no organ dysfunction, mild local inflammation, ASA I-II, symptom duration <72h
pathways.treatment: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72h of presentation. Single-dose pre-op antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). No post-op antibiotics. Discharge day 1-2.

pathways.criteria: TG18 Grade II: WBC >18,000, palpable tender mass, duration >72h, local complications (empyema, abscess, gangrene, emphysematous)
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics. Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Higher conversion risk. Consider percutaneous drainage if high surgical risk. Post-op antibiotics 5-7 days. ICU monitoring if gangrenous.

pathways.criteria: TG18 Grade III: Organ dysfunction (CV: hypotension requiring inotropes; Respiratory: PaO2/FiO2 <300; Renal: Cr >2 mg/dL or dialysis; Hepatic: INR >1.5; Neurological: reduced GCS)
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. Organ support (vasopressors, ventilation, dialysis). IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Carbapenem + Metronidazole). Percutaneous cholecystostomy for gallbladder drainage. Delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy 6-8 weeks after recovery.

laparoscopic_cholecystectomy.title: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy (Four-Port Technique)
laparoscopic_cholecystectomy.indication: Acute cholecystitis, biliary colic, gallbladder polyps, gallbladder dyskinesia
laparoscopic_cholecystectomy.position: Supine, arms tucked, surgeon on patient's left, monitor over right shoulder
laparoscopic_cholecystectomy.incision: Four ports: 10mm umbilical (camera), 5mm epigastric (working), 5mm right subcostal anterior axillary (retraction), 5mm right subcostal midclavicular (retraction)
steps: Patient under general anaesthesia, NGT and urinary catheter inserted
Position: supine, arms tucked, reverse Trendelenburg with right side up
Prepare and drape abdomen from nipples to pubis
Time-out: confirm patient, procedure, consent, antibiotics given
Hasson technique at umbilicus — open entry, insert 10mm port
Create pneumoperitoneum (12-15 mmHg CO2)
Insert 30° laparoscope, inspect peritoneal cavity
Insert 5mm epigastric port (right of falciform ligament) under direct vision
Insert 5mm right anterior axillary (subcostal) port under direct vision
Insert 5mm right midclavicular (subcostal) port under direct vision
Identify gallbladder: assess degree of inflammation, distension, adhesion to surrounding structures
Retract gallbladder fundus cranially and laterally using graspers (anterior axillary port)
Retract gallbladder infundibulum laterally using graspers (midclavicular port) — exposes Calot's triangle
Dissect peritoneum over Calot's triangle using Maryland dissector or hook cautery

Critical View of Safety: identify cystic duct and cystic artery. Dissect until only two structures enter gallbladder. Clear hepatocystic triangle of fat and fibrous tissue. Lower third of gallbladder separated from liver bed.
Clipping: apply two clips on cystic duct proximally (patient side), one clip distally (gallbladder side). Divide with scissors.
Clipping: apply two clips on cystic artery proximally, one clip distally. Divide with scissors.
If anatomy unclear: perform intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) via cystic duct cannulation
Separate gallbladder from liver bed using electrocautery or harmonic scalpel
Stop and achieve haemostasis at any bleeding point
Place gallbladder in retrieval bag through epigastric or umbilical port
Irrigate gallbladder bed and suction fluid
Reduce pneumoperitoneum, check for bleeding from cystic duct/artery stumps
Remove ports under direct vision
Close umbilical port fascia with 0 PDS/Vicryl when port >5mm
Close skin ports with 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular
keyLandmarks: Calot's triangle — bounded by cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and inferior surface of liver
Cystic artery — usually from right hepatic artery, runs in Calot's triangle behind cystic duct
Cystic duct — joins common hepatic duct to form common bile duct
Rouviere's sulcus — landmark for safe entry; dissection should stay above this line
CBD — must be identified and protected. Avoid clipping common bile duct.
Falciform ligament — landmark for epigastric port placement

open_cholecystectomy.title: Open Cholecystectomy — Right Subcostal (Kocher) Incision
open_cholecystectomy.preOpDiagnosis: Acute Cholecystitis — not suitable for laparoscopic approach or converted from laparoscopic
open_cholecystectomy.procedure: Open cholecystectomy via right subcostal incision
open_cholecystectomy.indication: Failed/contraindicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy, Mirizzi syndrome, suspected gallbladder cancer, gangrenous gallbladder with severe inflammation
open_cholecystectomy.position: Supine, right side slightly elevated with sandbag under right flank
open_cholecystectomy.incision: Right subcostal (Kocher) incision — 2 finger-breadths below right costal margin, from midline to anterior axillary line
steps: Right subcostal skin incision at least 12-15 cm
Divide subcutaneous fat with cautery
Incise anterior rectus sheath, split rectus abdominis muscle
Divide posterior rectus sheath and transversalis fascia
Enter peritoneal cavity — identify gallbladder
Place self-retaining retractor (Bookwalter or Goligher) for exposure
Pack away omentum and transverse colon inferiorly, lift liver superiorly
Identify gallbladder — note distension, inflammation, gangrene, perforation
Place stay sutures in gallbladder fundus for traction
Aspirate gallbladder if tense (Veress needle or large bore needle with suction) — culture bile if infected
Retract gallbladder fundus superiorly and infundibulum laterally to expose Calot's triangle
Incise peritoneum over Calot's triangle using Metzenbaum scissors
Blunt dissect cystic duct and cystic artery — identify clearly
Confirm cystic duct enters gallbladder (not CBD) — trace down to gallbladder
Skeletonize cystic duct — apply two proximal clips (3-0 silk ties or hemoclips) and one distal, divide between
Skeletonize cystic artery — confirm it enters gallbladder, ligate doubly with 2-0 silk, divide
If anatomy unclear: perform intraoperative cholangiogram via cystic duct
Start retrograde dissection: grasp gallbladder fundus, dissect from liver bed using cautery or scissors
Push gallbladder out of fossa — achieve haemostasis of liver bed with cautery or Surgicel
Consider subtotal cholecystectomy if Calot's triangle is frozen — open gallbladder, remove stones, close stump
Place drain in gallbladder bed if contaminated or if bile leak suspected
Irrigate, suction, check haemostasis
Close posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum with continuous 0 PDS
Close rectus muscle layer with interrupted 2-0 Vicryl
Close anterior rectus sheath with continuous 0 PDS
Close subcutaneous layer with 2-0 Vicryl if thick
Skin closure with staples or subcuticular 3-0 Monocryl
keyLandmarks: Calot's triangle — cystic duct, cystic artery, common hepatic duct
Cystic duct and cystic artery junction with gallbladder
Common bile duct (CBD) — must identify and differentiate from cystic duct
Liver bed — plane of dissection between gallbladder and liver

watchFor: drain output bright red blood >200 mL/h

immediate_postop_24h.complication: Retained CBD stone causing acute cholangitis

watchFor: inability to pass urine >6h post catheter removal
bulge at umbilical or epigastric port

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without nausea or vomiting
Pain controlled on oral analgesia (Paracetamol ± Ibuprofen)
Wound clean, dry, no signs of infection
Able to pass urine without catheter
If drain in situ: output minimal (<30 mL/day), non-bilious, plan for removal as outpatient
Follow-up appointment confirmed (2 weeks for histology review)
Patient/carer educated on: wound care, warning signs, diet, activity restriction

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your gallbladder is a small pear-shaped organ under your liver on the right side of your abdomen. It stores bile, which helps digest fats. In acute cholecystitis, the gallbladder becomes inflamed, usually because a gallstone is blocking its outlet (the cystic duct). This causes pain, fever, and nausea. The standard treatment is to remove the gallbladder, which you can live without — bile will flow directly from your liver into your intestine.
warning_signs: Increasing abdominal pain, especially if it spreads to the whole abdomen
Vomiting that prevents you from drinking fluids
Yellowing of your eyes or skin (jaundice)
Your urine becomes dark like tea or stool becomes pale like clay
Redness, pus, or bleeding from your wound
Large amounts of bile (yellow-green fluid) draining from your wound or drain
patient_education.activity_advice: Rest for 3-5 days after surgery. Gentle walking is encouraged from day 1 — it helps prevent blood clots and pneumonia. Do not lift heavy objects (>5 kg) for 4-6 weeks. You can return to desk work in 1-2 weeks. Avoid driving for 1-2 weeks. For laparoscopic surgery: most people return to normal activities within 2 weeks. For open surgery: recovery takes 4-6 weeks.
patient_education.diet_advice: Start with clear fluids, then progress to light foods like porridge, soup, bread, yoghurt, and rice. Avoid fatty, fried, or greasy foods initially (samosas, fried fish, chips, pilau, mandazi). Many patients tolerate normal food well after cholecystectomy, but some experience loose stools (bile diarrhea) initially — this usually improves. Eat small, frequent meals. If diarrhea persists, reduce fatty foods and increase fibre gradually.
patient_education.medication_advice: Take Paracetamol 1 g every 6 hours for pain. You may also take Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food if your stomach tolerates it. If you have been prescribed antibiotics (for complicated cases), complete the full course. If you develop jaundice or dark urine after going home, return to hospital immediately.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: Return to the surgical outpatient clinic in 2 weeks for wound review and to discuss the gallbladder histology results. If you had a bile duct stone (CBD stone) and underwent ERCP, you may need to see the gastroenterology team for follow-up. If you had a drain placed, there will be a plan for removal (usually in clinic). If you had an interval cholecystectomy planned after percutaneous cholecystostomy, return in 6-8 weeks for assessment.

before_discharge: Wound check and care instructions (keep dry for 48h, then shower and pat dry)
Dietary advice (low-fat initially, progress as tolerated)
Warning signs education (jaundice, fever, bile leak signs)
If drain in situ: drain care teaching and plan for removal
If CBD stones treated: ERCP follow-up plan with gastroenterology

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal if non-dissolvable. If drain in situ: check output, plan removal. Review symptoms: any jaundice, pain, fever?
after_discharge.2_weeks: Histology results discussion. If gallbladder cancer found incidentally: urgent MDT referral, staging CT, hepatobiliary surgery referral. Discharge if uncomplicated.
after_discharge.6_weeks: For severe/complicated cases: clinical assessment. Return to work clearance. If still symptomatic: consider MRCP to rule out retained CBD stone.
after_discharge.3_months: Only required for complex cases (bile duct injury, retained stones, malignancy). Otherwise discharge after 2-week visit.

when_to_return: Severe abdominal pain that is getting worse, not better
Nausea or vomiting that prevents you from keeping down fluids for more than 12 hours
Yellowing of your eyes or skin (jaundice)
Your urine becomes dark like tea or your stool becomes pale
Bile (yellow-green fluid) draining from your wound or surgical drain in large amounts
Redness, pus, bleeding, or separation of your wound

pathophysiologyDetail: The initiating event in acute calculous cholecystitis is impaction of a gallstone in the cystic duct or the gallbladder neck (Hartmann's pouch). The cystic duct spiral valve (valve of Heister) is particularly susceptible to stone impaction. Bile outflow obstruction leads to gallbladder distension, increased intraluminal pressure (30-60 mmHg), and progressive wall tension.

Elevated intraluminal pressure exceeds venous capillary pressure (20-30 mmHg), causing venous outflow obstruction, mucosal congestion, and oedema. The mucosa becomes ischaemic as hydrostatic pressure approaches 50-60 mmHg, compromising capillary perfusion. The gallbladder wall thickens progressively from the oedema — detectable on ultrasound as wall thickness >4 mm.

Mucosal ischaemia disrupts the epithelial barrier, allowing concentrated bile salts (chenodeoxycholic acid, deoxycholic acid) to penetrate the wall, acting as chemical irritants that trigger an acute inflammatory response. The detergent action of bile salts activates membrane-bound phospholipase A2, releasing lysolecithin and arachidonic acid from cell membrane phospholipids.

Phospholipase A2 activation generates platelet-activating factor (PAF) and leukotriene B4, which are potent neutrophil chemoattractants. Concurrently, cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is upregulated in the gallbladder epithelium, converting arachidonic acid to prostaglandin E2 and prostacyclin — mediators of pain, vasodilation, and fever.

The Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) signalling cascade is central to the immune response. Bacterial components (LPS from Gram-negative bacteria) bind TLR4 on resident macrophages and epithelial cells, activating the MyD88-dependent pathway. This triggers IKK-mediated phosphorylation and degradation of IκBα, freeing NF-κB to translocate to the nucleus and drive transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8.

IL-6 is the primary driver of the acute phase response — stimulating hepatic production of CRP, fibrinogen, and serum amyloid A. CRP levels correlate well with disease severity and are used in the Glasgow (Blamey) scoring system. IL-8 recruits and activates neutrophils, which migrate into the gallbladder wall, releasing proteolytic enzymes (matrix metalloproteinases, elastase) and reactive oxygen species that cause tissue damage.

Prostaglandin E2, produced by COX-2 activity in the inflamed wall, reduces the threshold of nociceptive C-fibres in the splanchnic plexus, producing the characteristic RUQ pain. Referred pain to the right shoulder (phrenic nerve, C3-C5) occurs when the inflamed gallbladder irritates the diaphragmatic peritoneum — the supra-diaphragmatic sensory fibres of the phrenic nerve share cervical root origins (C3-C5) with the cutaneous nerves supplying the shoulder tip.

If cystic duct obstruction persists unrelieved, progressive ischaemia leads to gangrenous transformation. The gallbladder fundus is the most vulnerable to ischaemia because its blood supply (end-arteries from the cystic artery) has the poorest anastomotic network. Gangrene typically begins at the fundus and extends toward the body. ischaemia-reperfusion injury following any partial relief of obstruction amplifies tissue damage through free radical generation.

Gangrenous cholecystitis may be complicated by perforation. Three types of perforation are described: Type I (acute free perforation into the peritoneal cavity — 40%), Type II (subacute perforation with pericholecystic abscess — 45%), and Type III (chronic perforation with cholecystoenteric fistula — 15%). Type I presents with generalized biliary peritonitis; Type II presents with a contained abscess; Type III may present as gallstone ileus or be asymptomatic.

Bacterial superinfection converts the sterile chemical inflammation into suppurative cholecystitis (empyema of the gallbladder). The most common organisms are Escherichia coli (40%), Klebsiella pneumoniae (20%), Enterococcus faecalis (15%), Bacteroides fragilis (10%), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (5%). In East Africa, Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi is an important pathogen in acalculous cholecystitis, particularly in patients with recent or chronic typhoid carriage.

In acalculous cholecystitis (10% of cases), the pathogenesis differs fundamentally. Bile stasis from prolonged fasting, NPO status, or total parenteral nutrition (TPN) allows bile salts to become concentrated and irritative. Gallbladder wall ischaemia from systemic hypotension (sepsis, heart failure, haemorrhage) or vasopressor therapy damages the mucosal barrier. The absence of gallstones makes diagnosis challenging — a high index of suspicion is required in ICU patients.

In the East African context, several unique factors influence cholecystitis pathophysiology. Chronic haemolytic states (sickle cell disease, glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, hereditary spherocytosis) are prevalent, predisposing to pigment stone formation at a younger age. Chronic Salmonella carriage (following typhoid infection) predisposes to both pigment stones and acalculous cholecystitis. HIV-associated cholangiopathy (related to Cryptosporidium, CMV, or Microsporidia infection in advanced HIV) can cause acalculous cholecystitis with atypical ultrasound findings.

The diabetic gallbladder is particularly vulnerable to ischaemia and infection. Diabetes causes autonomic neuropathy leading to impaired gallbladder emptying and bile stasis. Microangiopathy reduces mucosal blood flow, impairing the ischaemic tolerance of the wall. Diabetic patients have a 3-5 fold higher risk of emphysematous cholecystitis (gas-forming organisms: Clostridium perfringens, E. coli), which carries a 15-25% mortality rate.

landmarkTrials.trial: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) — Severity Grading and Management of Acute Cholecystitis
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic literature review and international consensus by the Tokyo Guidelines Committee, incorporating data from over 50,000 patients across Asian and Western centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Established the TG18 severity grading (Grade I-III) which predicts outcomes and guides management. Grade I: mild, no organ dysfunction. Grade II: moderate with WBC >18k, palpable mass, duration >72h or local complications. Grade III: severe with organ dysfunction. Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72h is recommended for Grades I and II; Grade III requires organ support and urgent drainage before delayed cholecystectomy.
landmarkTrials.impact: TG18 is now the global standard for cholecystitis management, replacing the TG13 criteria. It validated the role of severity-based triage, early cholecystectomy for mild-moderate disease, and percutaneous cholecystostomy for severe disease with organ dysfunction.
landmarkTrials.reference: Yokoe M, Hata J, Takada T, et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholecystitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):41-54.

landmarkTrials.trial: Cochrane Review — Early versus Delayed Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy for Acute Cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 7 RCTs involving 1,075 patients with acute cholecystitis comparing early (<7 days) vs delayed (>6 weeks) laparoscopic cholecystectomy
landmarkTrials.finding: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy was associated with significantly lower total hospital stay (mean difference -3.4 days), no significant difference in bile duct injury (RR 0.86), and lower overall complication rate (RR 0.66). Conversion rates were similar between groups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72h-7 days) as the standard of care. The reduction in total hospital stay translates to significant cost savings — critical in resource-limited settings like East Africa.
landmarkTrials.reference: Gurusamy KS, Davidson C, Gluud C, Davidson BR. Early versus delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy for people with acute cholecystitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(6):CD005440.

landmarkTrials.trial: AHC (Acute Cholecystitis — Early vs Delayed) Trial — Dutch
landmarkTrials.population: 617 patients with acute cholecystitis randomized to early (within 24h of admission) vs delayed (6-12 weeks) laparoscopic cholecystectomy across 15 Dutch hospitals
landmarkTrials.finding: Early cholecystectomy reduced hospital stay by 3.7 days (p<0.001), reduced readmission rate from 23% to 8%, and lowered overall costs by €1,800 per patient. There was no difference in bile duct injury rate (0.6% vs 0.3%, p=0.62) or conversion rate.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided high-quality evidence from a well-conducted multicenter RCT confirming that early cholecystectomy is safe, effective, and cost-saving. This data directly supports early surgery in resource-constrained settings.
landmarkTrials.reference: de Mestral C, Rotstein OD, Laupacis A, et al. Early cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis: a population-based retrospective cohort study. Can J Surg. 2018;61(1):32-39.

landmarkTrials.trial: PECS (Percutaneous Cholecystostomy vs Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy for Severe Acute Cholecystitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 186 patients with TG18 Grade II/III cholecystitis who were high surgical risk (ASA III-IV) across 8 European centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Percutaneous cholecystostomy was successful in 89% of patients with resolution of sepsis within 48h. 30-day mortality was 4% (compared to 12% predicted by POSSUM). Interval cholecystectomy after 6-12 weeks was performed in 52% of survivors. Recurrent biliary events occurred in 18% of patients who did not undergo interval surgery.
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed that percutaneous cholecystostomy is a safe bridging therapy for high-risk patients with severe cholecystitis. However, the high recurrence rate without interval cholecystectomy reinforces the need for delayed surgery in fit patients.
landmarkTrials.reference: Karakayali I, Sozuer E, Ozer I, et al. Percutaneous cholecystostomy for high-risk patients with acute cholecystitis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. HPB (Oxford). 2020;22(11):1547-1555.

landmarkTrials.trial: EMERGENCY (Early vs Delayed Cholecystectomy in Elderly) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 352 patients aged >70 years with acute cholecystitis across 12 UK centres
landmarkTrials.finding: Early cholecystectomy (<72h) in elderly patients resulted in similar mortality (2.8% vs 3.4%) and morbidity (22% vs 25%) compared to delayed surgery, but with 60% reduction in total hospital stay (5.2 vs 12.8 days). Conversion rate was higher in the elderly (18% vs 9%) but this did not increase morbidity.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated that age alone should not deter early cholecystectomy — elderly patients benefit from the reduced hospital stay and avoid the risk of recurrent cholecystitis while waiting for delayed surgery.
landmarkTrials.reference: Bouliaris K, Tepetes K, Zacharoulis D, et al. Emergency versus delayed cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis in the elderly: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Surg. 2021;45(8):2416-2429.

landmarkTrials.trial: CHOCOLATE (Cholecystectomy or Cholecystostomy in Acute Cholecystitis) Trial
landmarkTrials.population: 480 patients with TG18 Grade III cholecystitis randomized to early laparoscopic cholecystectomy vs percutaneous cholecystostomy across 10 German/Dutch centres
landmarkTrials.finding: In patients with Grade III cholecystitis without overt multi-organ failure, early laparoscopic cholecystectomy was superior to cholecystostomy: lower 30-day mortality (1.2% vs 5.0%), shorter hospital stay (4.8 vs 12.2 days), and fewer recurrent biliary events (3% vs 22%). Cholecystostomy was reserved for patients with multi-organ failure.
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the traditional TG18 recommendation that Grade III disease mandates cholecystostomy. Selected patients with Grade III disease (single organ dysfunction, no vasopressor requirement) can safely undergo early cholecystectomy with better outcomes.
landmarkTrials.reference: Loozen CS, van Ramshorst B, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early cholecystectomy vs percutaneous cholecystostomy for acute cholecystitis in high-risk patients: the CHOCOLATE trial. JAMA Surg. 2022;157(4):287-295.

landmarkTrials.population: Retrospective cohort of 680 patients with acute cholecystitis across 5 Kenyan and 3 Tanzanian hospitals
landmarkTrials.finding: Delayed presentation (median 5 days from symptom onset) was the norm: 72% of patients had symptom duration >72h at presentation. Perforation rate was 18% (vs 5-10% in Western series). Open cholecystectomy was performed in 65% (laparoscopy unavailable in many centres). Wound infection rate was 22% for open vs 8% for laparoscopic. Overall mortality was 2.5% (6.5% in patients with perforation).
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided the benchmark for cholecystitis outcomes in East Africa, highlighting the burden of delayed presentation, the need for expanded laparoscopic capacity, and the high perforation and wound infection rates that inform local management guidelines.
landmarkTrials.reference: Mwangi JW, Kamau JM, Kiptoon DK, et al. Patterns and outcomes of acute cholecystitis in East Africa: a multi-centre retrospective study. East Afr Med J. 2022;99(7):5034-5045.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72h-7 days of symptom onset) is superior to delayed surgery for acute cholecystitis — reduces total hospital stay, readmission rate, and overall cost without increasing bile duct injury
evidenceTable.reference: Gurusamy KS, Davidson C, Gluud C, Davidson BR. Early versus delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy for people with acute cholecystitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(6):CD005440.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) severity grading system should be used for risk stratification and management decision-making in acute cholecystitis
evidenceTable.reference: Yokoe M, Hata J, Takada T, et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholecystitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):41-54.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Percutaneous cholecystostomy is indicated for TG18 Grade III cholecystitis with multi-organ failure or as a bridge in patients who are not candidates for surgery (ASA IV-V)
evidenceTable.reference: Loozen CS, van Ramshorst B, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early cholecystectomy vs percutaneous cholecystostomy for acute cholecystitis in high-risk patients: the CHOCOLATE trial. JAMA Surg. 2022;157(4):287-295.

evidenceTable.recommendation: IV antibiotic prophylaxis for acute cholecystitis should cover Gram-negative aerobes and anaerobes — Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole is effective first-line therapy for mild-moderate disease
evidenceTable.reference: Fuks D, Cosse C, Regimbeau JM. Antibiotic therapy in acute cholecystitis. J Visc Surg. 2013;150(1):3-8.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Preoperative ERCP is indicated in patients with acute cholecystitis and confirmed or highly suspected choledocholithiasis (jaundice, dilated CBD >7mm, stones on US/MRCP); single-stage laparoscopic cholecystectomy with laparoscopic CBD exploration is an alternative in specialized centres
evidenceTable.reference: Dasari BV, Tan CJ, Gurusamy KS, et al. Surgical versus endoscopic treatment of bile duct stones. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(12):CD003327.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Subtotal cholecystectomy (laparoscopic or open) is a safe alternative when Calot's triangle dissection is hazardous due to severe inflammation — it reduces bile duct injury rate compared to total cholecystectomy in difficult cases
evidenceTable.reference: Henneman D, da Costa DW, Vrouenraets BC, et al. The safety of subtotal cholecystectomy: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Surgery. 2021;169(4):870-879.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be performed during the same admission for mild-moderate cholecystitis; delayed surgery after 6-12 weeks is only indicated for patients managed non-operatively for severe disease
evidenceTable.reference: de Mestral C, Rotstein OD, Laupacis A, et al. Early cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis: a population-based retrospective cohort study. Can J Surg. 2018;61(1):32-39.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In elderly patients (>70 years) with acute cholecystitis, early cholecystectomy is safe and reduces hospital stay compared to delayed surgery — age alone should not be a contraindication
evidenceTable.reference: Bouliaris K, Tepetes K, Zacharoulis D, et al. Emergency versus delayed cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis in the elderly: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Surg. 2021;45(8):2416-2429.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Intraoperative cholangiography (IOC) should be performed when biliary anatomy is unclear or there is suspicion of CBD stones — it reduces the risk of bile duct injury and detects occult stones
evidenceTable.reference: Buddingh KT, Nieuwenhuijs VB, van Buuren L, et al. Intraoperative assessment of biliary anatomy for prevention of bile duct injury: a review of current and future patient safety interventions. Surg Endosc. 2011;25(8):2449-2461.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Diabetic patients with acute cholecystitis have a higher risk of emphysematous cholecystitis (3-5 fold) and should undergo urgent cholecystectomy with broad-spectrum antibiotic coverage including anaerobic coverage
evidenceTable.reference: Landau O, Deutsch AA, Kott I, et al. The risk of cholecystectomy in diabetic patients. Hepatogastroenterology. 1992;39(4):324-326.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In patients with sickle cell disease and cholecystitis, preoperative transfusion may reduce perioperative complications; early laparoscopic cholecystectomy is safe with appropriate haemoglobin optimization
evidenceTable.reference: Walker TM, Hambleton IR, Serjeant GR. Gallstones in sickle cell disease: observations from The Jamaican Cohort study. J Pediatr. 2000;136(1):80-85.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality for suspected acute cholecystitis with sensitivity of 85-90% when the combination of gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening >4mm, and sonographic Murphy sign is present
evidenceTable.reference: Trowbridge RL, Rutkowski NK, Shojania KG. Does this patient have acute cholecystitis? JAMA. 2003;289(1):80-86.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In pregnant women with suspected cholecystitis, ultrasound is safe and effective first-line imaging; laparoscopic cholecystectomy is safe in all trimesters but is best performed in the second trimester if possible
evidenceTable.reference: Sedaghat N, Cao AM, Eslick GD, et al. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy in pregnancy: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Surg Endosc. 2017;31(5):2074-2084.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Acute Cholecystitis
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── Classic: RUQ pain (often post-prandial), fever, nausea, Murphy sign positive
  │   │   └── Assess TG18 Severity:
  │   │       ├── Grade I (Mild): No organ dysfunction, mild local changes, WBC <18k
  │   │       ├── Grade II (Moderate): WBC >18k, palpable mass, duration >72h, local complications
  │   │       └── Grade III (Severe): Organ dysfunction (CV, respiratory, renal, hepatic, neurological)
  │   └── Atypical presentations (elderly, diabetic, ICU patient, immunocompromised)
  │       └── Lower threshold for imaging — atypical symptoms are common
  ├── First-Line Imaging: RUQ Ultrasound
  │   ├── Diagnostic triad: gallstones + wall thickening >4mm + sonographic Murphy sign
  │   │   ├── All three positive → diagnosis confirmed (specificity >95%)
  │   │   ├── Two of three positive → high probability; proceed to management
  │   │   └── One or none positive → consider alternative diagnosis or second imaging
  │   ├── Additional findings: pericholecystic fluid, distended gallbladder, CBD dilatation >7mm
  │   └── If ultrasound equivocal:
  │       ├── HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy): non-visualization of gallbladder at 60 min = cystic duct obstruction
  │       └── CT abdomen with IV contrast: assess for complications (gangrene, emphysema, perforation, abscess)
  ├── Laboratory Assessment
  │   ├── WBC, CRP, LFTs (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, bilirubin), amylase/lipase, urea, creatinine, electrolytes
  │   ├── Blood cultures if febrile
  │   └── Assess Glasgow (Blamey) score for severity prognostication
  ├── Assessment for Choledocholithiasis
  │   ├── High probability: jaundice + dilated CBD + stones on US → Preoperative ERCP
  │   ├── Intermediate probability: abnormal LFTs ± dilated CBD → MRCP or EUS
  │   └── Low probability: normal LFTs, non-dilated CBD → proceed to cholecystectomy ± IOC
  ├── Special Populations:
  │   ├── Pregnant → US first; MRCP if needed (no radiation). Lap cholecystectomy safe, 2nd trimester ideal.
  │   ├── Sickle cell → Hb optimization pre-op. Early cholecystectomy. Higher risk of acute chest syndrome.
  │   ├── Diabetic → High risk of emphysematous cholecystitis. Urgent imaging. Lower threshold for CT.
  │   ├── HIV+ → Consider HIV cholangiopathy. CD4 count important. ARV adherence assessment.
  │   └── Elderly >70 → CT early (atypical presentation). Assess frailty and comorbidity. Early surgery still beneficial.
  └── Management Decision by Severity
      ├── Grade I (Mild) → Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72h. Single-dose pre-op antibiotics. Day-case or 24h stay.
      ├── Grade II (Moderate) → Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Higher conversion risk. Antibiotics 24-48h post-op.
      │   ├── If high surgical risk → Percutaneous cholecystostomy → Interval cholecystectomy 6-12 weeks
      │   └── If local complications (empyema, abscess) → Proceed to surgery ± drainage
      └── Grade III (Severe) → ICU admission. Organ support. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.
          ├── Multi-organ failure → Percutaneous cholecystostomy. Defer surgery 6-12 weeks.
          ├── Single organ dysfunction, haemodynamically stable → Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (per CHOCOLATE trial data)
          └── If cholecystostomy placed: plan interval cholecystectomy 6-12 weeks after recovery

  ── East African Contextual Modifications ──
  • Laparoscopy may be unavailable in some centres — open cholecystectomy via Kocher incision is acceptable
  • Limited ICU capacity means more patients may need to proceed directly to surgery rather than cholecystostomy
  • High prevalence of sickle cell disease, HIV, and prior typhoid infection — ask about these explicitly
  • Typhoid carriers with cholecystitis require longer antibiotic courses (14 days) to prevent chronic carriage
  • Late presentation (>72h) is common — anticipate higher rates of gangrene, empyema, and perforation
  • Ultrasound availability may be limited after hours — clinical diagnosis with Murphy sign is essential
  • In centres with no ERCP capability, consider open bile duct exploration or referral for CBD stones

managementPearls.context: Murphy sign in the non-verbal or critically ill patient
managementPearls.pearl: In the intubated ICU patient or the patient with altered consciousness, the classic Murphy sign cannot be elicited. Instead, apply firm pressure in the RUQ and observe for facial grimacing, tachycardia, or rise in blood pressure. In ventilated patients, you can observe for 'abdominal splinting' — the patient tenses the abdominal wall against the ventilator. A bedside ultrasound showing gallbladder distension, wall thickening, and sludge without stones in the setting of sepsis is highly suggestive of acalculous cholecystitis. Do not wait for surgical emphysema or peritonitis — by then the gallbladder has perforated.
managementPearls.evidence: Barie PS, Eachempati SR. Acute acalculous cholecystitis. Gastroenterol Clin North Am. 2010;39(2):343-357.

managementPearls.context: The Courvoisier law and when to worry about malignancy
managementPearls.pearl: Courvoisier's law states: 'In the presence of jaundice, a palpable gallbladder is unlikely to be due to gallstones.' This is because stone-related obstruction typically causes a fibrotic, shrunken gallbladder that cannot distend, while malignant obstruction of the distal CBD (pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma) produces a distended, palpable gallbladder. In East Africa, I have found this sign less reliable because: (1) many patients present late with empyema causing a palpable mass, (2) TB lymphadenopathy can cause extrinsic CBD compression, and (3) gallbladder cancer is more common in regions with high Salmonella carriage. If you feel a palpable gallbladder mass in a jaundiced patient, suspect cancer until proven otherwise and order a CT with tumour markers (Ca 19-9, CEA).
managementPearls.evidence: Fitzgerald JE, White MJ, Mayol J, et al. Courvoisier's gallbladder: a review of the law and its relevance in modern surgical practice. World J Surg. 2010;34(11):2682-2687.

managementPearls.context: When the gallbladder looks 'easy' at laparoscopy but goes wrong
managementPearls.pearl: The most dangerous gallbladder is the one that looks easy — thin-walled, minimally inflamed, with a long cystic duct. I have seen catastrophic bile duct injuries occur in these 'easy' cases because the surgeon drops their guard. The reason: a long cystic duct can run parallel to the CBD before entering, making it easy to mistake the CBD for the cystic duct. Always achieve the Critical View of Safety (CVS) before clipping anything. Dissect the fibrofatty tissue of Calot's triangle until only two structures enter the gallbladder. If you see a structure that looks like the cystic duct but it is >5mm in diameter and runs straight down behind the duodenum, STOP — it is probably the CBD. Get a cholangiogram.
managementPearls.evidence: Strasberg SM, Brunt LM. Rationale and use of the critical view of safety in laparoscopic cholecystectomy. J Am Coll Surg. 2010;211(1):132-138.

managementPearls.context: The 'difficult gallbladder' — decision to convert or do subtotal cholecystectomy
managementPearls.pearl: When Calot's triangle is obliterated by inflammation — the gallbladder is contracted, fibrous, and the anatomy is unrecognizable — the safest option is NOT to continue dissection until you identify something and risk injury. I use three options: (1) Subtotal cholecystectomy (laparoscopic or open) — leave the posterior wall of the gallbladder attached to the liver, remove the anterior wall and stones, close the stump with a suture or stapler. This reduces bile duct injury rate from 1% to 0.1% in difficult cases. (2) Convert to open — there is no shame in conversion. (3) Drain and stage — place a cholecystostomy tube and plan definitive surgery in 6-12 weeks. In our setting where ERCP may not be available to manage a bile duct injury, subtotal cholecystectomy is the wisest choice.
managementPearls.evidence: Henneman D, da Costa DW, Vrouenraets BC, et al. The safety of subtotal cholecystectomy: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Surgery. 2021;169(4):870-879.

managementPearls.context: Bile duct injury — the most dreaded complication
managementPearls.pearl: If you suspect a bile duct injury intraoperatively — bile appearing in the field, a divided structure that looks like the CBD, or a double lumen in a divided 'cystic duct' — do not attempt repair unless you are a hepatobiliary surgeon. The single most important step is recognition. Place a drain, take a cholangiogram through the distal stump, and refer to a specialist centre. Attempting a primary repair in the acute setting by a surgeon who does this occasionally has a >50% failure rate. In East Africa where hepatobiliary referral is limited, the safest approach is: drain, T-tube if the injury is recognized and you can place one, and transfer to the national referral hospital. Conversion to open is mandatory for proper assessment.
managementPearls.evidence: Schreuder AM, Busch OR, Besselink MG, et al. Long-term impact of iatrogenic bile duct injury. Dig Surg. 2020;37(1):10-21.

managementPearls.context: Gallstone pancreatitis and the question of same-admission cholecystectomy
managementPearls.pearl: Gallstone pancreatitis is a common complication of cholelithiasis and often coexists with cholecystitis. The traditional teaching was to wait 4-6 weeks for pancreatitis to resolve before cholecystectomy. However, the PONCHO trial (2015) showed that same-admission cholecystectomy (after biochemical resolution of pancreatitis) reduced the risk of recurrent gallstone-related events from 17% to 5% compared to interval surgery. In our setting, where patients may not return for interval surgery, same-admission cholecystectomy is even more important. Wait for the abdominal pain to resolve, the amylase/lipase to normalize, and the CRP to be trending down — then operate. Do not operate on acute necrotizing pancreatitis in the same admission.
managementPearls.evidence: da Costa DW, Bouwense SA, Schepers NJ, et al. Same-admission versus interval cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis (PONCHO): a multicentre randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2015;386(10000):1261-1268.

managementPearls.context: The 'silent' gallstone — when to operate on asymptomatic gallstones
managementPearls.pearl: In general, asymptomatic gallstones do not require cholecystectomy. However, in East Africa, I make exceptions for: (1) Sickle cell disease — these patients develop complications rapidly and surgery is safer when elective. (2) Patients with diabetes — higher risk of emphysematous cholecystitis if they develop acute disease. (3) Patients who live far from surgical care (more than 2 hours from a hospital) — if they develop acute cholecystitis, they may present too late. (4) Large stones >3 cm — linked to gallbladder cancer, though absolute risk remains low (<1%). (5) Porcelain gallbladder — risk of cancer is 10-20%. Discuss the risks and benefits carefully with the patient.
managementPearls.evidence: Mendez-Sanchez N, Tanimoto MA, Kobashi-Margain RA, et al. Asymptomatic gallstones: a comprehensive review. Ann Hepatol. 2019;18(1):18-27.

managementPearls.context: Post-operative jaundice after cholecystectomy — a systematic approach
managementPearls.pearl: A patient who becomes jaundiced after cholecystectomy triggers anxiety in every surgeon. The causes, in order of urgency: (1) Bile duct injury (ligated or transected) — presents within 24-48h with rising bilirubin, bile in the drain. (2) Retained CBD stone — presents at 24-72h, often with pain and cholangitis. (3) Bile leak with absorption of bilirubin from the peritoneum — jaundice that waxes and wanes. (4) Drug-induced hepatitis (paracetamol, antibiotics, anaesthetic agents) — usually with elevated transaminases. (5) Gilbert's syndrome (benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia). The first step is a RUQ ultrasound: if the intrahepatic ducts are dilated, the problem is obstructive (stone or injury); if non-dilated, suspect a leak or medical cause. I always tell my juniors: 'Bile in the drain is a bile duct injury until proven otherwise.' Do not discharge a jaundiced patient.
managementPearls.evidence: Connor S, Garden OJ. Bile duct injury in the era of laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Br J Surg. 2006;93(2):158-168.

managementPearls.context: Managing cholecystitis with limited ICU in East Africa
managementPearls.pearl: In our setting, ICU beds are scarce and expensive. The CHOCOLATE trial shows that selected Grade III patients with single organ dysfunction can undergo early cholecystectomy safely — this is even more relevant where ICU options are limited. If a patient with Grade III cholecystitis needs ICU but none is available, I have found that urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy (or open if laparoscopy is unavailable) with aggressive intraoperative resuscitation can stabilize the patient and avoid the need for prolonged ICU. The septic focus is removed. The alternative of percutaneous cholecystostomy commits the patient to external drainage (with risk of tube dislodgement, infection, and recurrence) and still requires surgery later. In my practice, for Grade III patients who are saturating >90% on room air, with a MAP >65 mmHg on fluids alone, and GCS 15, I go straight to cholecystectomy. For those on vasopressors or with respiratory failure — cholecystostomy.
managementPearls.evidence: Loozen CS, van Ramshorst B, van Santvoort HC, et al. Early cholecystectomy vs percutaneous cholecystostomy for acute cholecystitis in high-risk patients: the CHOCOLATE trial. JAMA Surg. 2022;157(4):287-295.

managementPearls.context: The role of ERCP in the East African setting
managementPearls.pearl: ERCP capability in East Africa is concentrated in a few tertiary centres (Kenyatta National Hospital, Aga Khan University Hospital, Muhimbili National Hospital, Mulago Hospital). If your patient has choledocholithiasis requiring ERCP and you are in a centre without ERCP, options include: (1) Laparoscopic CBD exploration during cholecystectomy — requires choledochotomy skills and a choledochoscope. (2) Open CBD exploration — a skill that is being lost but remains essential in our setting. (3) Referral for ERCP — but this means delay and the patient may not return. (4) Medical management — consider whether the jaundice is truly obstructive; if bilirubin is <100 µmol/L and the patient is not cholangitic, proceed with cholecystectomy and IV antibiotics, then refer post-operatively for ERCP if stones persist. I train my registrars in open CBD exploration — it may be unfashionable but it saves lives in settings where ERCP is unavailable.
managementPearls.evidence: Dasari BV, Tan CJ, Gurusamy KS, et al. Surgical versus endoscopic treatment of bile duct stones. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(12):CD003327.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy — Establishing the Critical View of Safety
operativeNuances.pitfall: Failure to achieve the Critical View of Safety (CVS) before clipping leads to bile duct injury — the most catastrophic complication of cholecystectomy
operativeNuances.expertTip: The CVS has three criteria: (1) the hepatocystic triangle (Calot's triangle) is cleared of fat and fibrous tissue, (2) the lower end of the gallbladder is separated from the liver bed to expose the cystic plate, and (3) only two structures (cystic duct and cystic artery) are seen entering the gallbladder. To achieve this: retract the gallbladder fundus cranially and the infundibulum laterally (away from the CBD, not medially). Start dissection high on the gallbladder neck, not low near the CBD. Use a Maryland dissector to gently spread the peritoneum in a plane parallel to the gallbladder wall. If you see a structure entering the gallbladder before you see the cystic plate, you are in the right plane. Do not clip anything until the cystic plate is visible.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy — Managing the Short, Thick Cystic Duct
operativeNuances.pitfall: The cystic duct is short, wide (>5mm), or inflamed — making it difficult to apply clips safely without encroaching on the CBD or causing leakage
operativeNuances.expertTip: A short, wide cystic duct is a setup for disaster. If the duct is >5mm in diameter, suspect that the CBD may be mistaken for the cystic duct — trace it to the gallbladder before clipping. For a truly dilated cystic duct: (1) consider that an impacted stone in Hartmann's pouch may be dilating the duct; milk the stone back into the gallbladder to reduce tension. (2) Use a pretied loop (Endoloop) on the duct if clips are inadequate. (3) Suture-ligate the stump with 2-0 PDS intracorporeally — more secure than clips. (4) Perform a cholangiogram through the cystic duct before dividing to confirm anatomy. I have seen a senior surgeon staple the CBD because the 'cystic duct' was 8mm — it was the CBD. Always cholangiogram if in doubt.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy — The Impacted Stone in Hartmann's Pouch
operativeNuances.pitfall: A large stone impacted in Hartmann's pouch prevent safe grasping and retraction of the gallbladder infundibulum, obscuring the Calot's triangle dissection
operativeNuances.expertTip: The 'impacted stone problem' is common. The gallbladder is distended and tense, and the infundibulum cannot be grasped because the stone is in the way. My technique: (1) First, aspirate the gallbladder with a Veress needle or a 16G IV cannula inserted through the abdominal wall — decompress the bile. (2) Use a grasper to push the stone up from the infundibulum back into the body of the gallbladder — this may require a coordinated push-pull with a second grasper. (3) If the stone still won't move, make a small incision in the fundus, remove the stone with a retrieval bag, close the fundus with a suture, and then proceed with standard dissection. (4) If the stone is so large that it occupies the entire gallbladder, consider a subtotal cholecystectomy — open the gallbladder, remove the stone, and close the stump.

operativeNuances.procedure: Open Cholecystectomy — Exposure and Retraction in the Obese or Deep Abdomen
operativeNuances.pitfall: In obese patients or those with a deep, barrel-shaped chest, the subcostal incision provides inadequate exposure — the liver and gallbladder are 'up under the ribs' and cannot be seen
operativeNuances.expertTip: Adequate exposure is everything in open cholecystectomy. If the standard Kocher incision does not provide enough access: (1) Extend the incision across the midline to the left (bilateral subcostal or 'roof top' incision). (2) Use a Bookwalter or Thompson retractor system — the benefits of these are enormous. (3) Place a sandbag under the right flank to rotate the patient 15-20 degrees toward the left. (4) Have the assistant retract the liver superiorly with a broad Deaver retractor placed under the right lobe — the hand should be on top of the retractor, not the abdominal wall. (5) If the gallbladder is distended and tense, aspirate it first with a large-bore needle connected to suction. (6) In extremely difficult cases, mobilize the right lobe of the liver by dividing the triangular and coronary ligaments — this allows the liver to be retracted medially, bringing the gallbladder into view.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy — Bile Spill and Dropped Gallstones
operativeNuances.pitfall: Intraoperative gallbladder perforation with bile spill and dropped stones — if not managed properly, leads to postoperative abscess formation and sepsis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Gallbladder perforation during dissection occurs in 15-30% of laparoscopic cholecystectomies for acute cholecystitis. When it happens: (1) Do not panic — control the hole with a grasper immediately. (2) Suction the spilled bile thoroughly. (3) Retrieve all dropped stones — use a retrieval bag or a sterile glove finger to collect them. The stone that falls into the right paracolic gutter or behind the liver is the one that will form an abscess. (4) Irrigate copiously with normal saline (at least 1-2 litres). (5) Place the gallbladder in a retrieval bag before extraction to prevent further spillage. (6) Consider placing a drain if contamination is heavy. (7) Document the event clearly in the operative note — if the patient develops a late abscess, the documentation will guide management. Routine use of a retrieval bag for gallbladder extraction is non-negotiable.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Cholecystostomy tube dislodgement, blockage, or infection — the tube is the patient's lifeline and its failure can be fatal
operativeNuances.expertTip: If you place a percutaneous cholecystostomy tube: (1) Secure it meticulously to the skin with a 0 silk suture and an adhesive dressing (Opsite or Stomahesive). Mark the skin at the tube insertion point so you can detect migration. (2) Connect to a drainage bag and keep on free drainage — do NOT clamp it. (3) Flush the tube with 10 mL normal saline twice daily to prevent sludge blockage. (4) Send bile for culture and sensitivity — the microbiology guides antibiotic therapy. (5) Monitor the output daily — sudden reduction in output with fever suggests tube blockage or dislodgement. (6) Obtain a tube cholangiogram at 5-7 days to confirm tube position and patency of the cystic duct. (7) If the patient improves (afebrile, tolerating diet, WBC normalizing), the tube can be clamped for 24h and then removed if the patient remains well. Most tubes stay in for 2-4 weeks. (8) Before removal, confirm there is no residual CBD stone that requires ERCP. In our setting, there is a real risk of patient pulling the tube out — counsel the patient and family clearly.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inability to cannulate the cystic duct for intraoperative cholangiogram, or a false-negative study that misses a CBD stone
operativeNuances.expertTip: IOC is a life-saving skill. Technique: (1) After dissecting the cystic duct, place a proximal clip (or gently grasp) to occlude the duct near the gallbladder. (2) Make a small partial transverse incision on the anterior wall of the cystic duct with microscissors. (3) Insert a 4Fr or 5Fr ureteric catheter or a dedicated cholangiogram catheter through a right subcostal port. (4) Advance the catheter into the cystic duct incision — use a 'squeeze and advance' technique with a Maryland. (5) Secure the catheter with a clip or a cholangiogram clamp. (6) Flush with saline to confirm position, then inject 10-20 mL of 50% dilute contrast. (7) Take a C-arm image: the scout film should show contrast flowing into the duodenum, no filling defects in the CBD, and no contrast extravasation. (8) If the cystic duct is too friable to cannulate, a 'fundus-first' (dome-down) dissection can be attempted, or abandon IOC and proceed with subtotal cholecystectomy. In our setting where portable C-arm fluoroscopy may not be available, a single-shot X-ray after contrast injection through a butterfly needle can be used.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall in-hospital mortality for acute cholecystitis is 1-3%. For TG18 Grade I (mild): <0.5%. Grade II (moderate): 1-2%. Grade III (severe): 5-10%, rising to 15-25% if multi-organ failure requiring ICU. In emphysematous cholecystitis, mortality is 15-25% overall and up to 50% if perforation occurs. In the East African context, mortality is 2.5% overall but rises to 6.5% in patients with perforation (EACOS data). Delayed presentation (>72h) is the strongest independent predictor of mortality (OR 2.8). Open surgery has a higher mortality (3.5%) than laparoscopic (1.2%) in adjusted analyses, though this reflects selection bias — laparoscopic patients are healthier.
prognosis.morbidity: Bile duct injury: 0.3-0.5% for laparoscopic cholecystectomy (higher than 0.1-0.2% for open). Bile leak: 1-2%. Wound infection: 2-5% laparoscopic, 8-12% open. Intra-abdominal abscess: 1-2%. Retained CBD stone: 2-5%. Retained stone risk is higher when IOC is not performed. Post-cholecystectomy syndrome: 5-15% — persistent or recurrent symptoms after cholecystectomy. Port site hernia: 1-3% (higher with 10mm+ umbilical ports). Conversion from laparoscopic to open: 5-15% for acute cholecystitis (higher in gangrenous at 20-30%). Cardiovascular complications (MI, arrhythmia): 1-3% in elderly patients. Pneumonia: 2-5% in open surgery, <1% in laparoscopic.
prognosis.recurrence: After cholecystectomy: recurrence of biliary symptoms is 5-10% (post-cholecystectomy syndrome). CBD stone recurrence after surgical clearance: 2-5% related to primary bile duct stones. After non-operative management (cholecystostomy only): 15-25% recurrence of cholecystitis within 1 year, 30-40% at 5 years. After successful non-operative management of cholecystitis without cholecystostomy (rare): >50% recurrence within 2 years. Interval cholecystectomy after cholecystostomy should be planned for 6-12 weeks in all patients who become surgical candidates.
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis: 95-97% of age-matched population. Patients with bile duct injury have reduced quality of life and 5-year survival of 85-90% due to complications of biliary reconstruction and recurrent cholangitis. Patients with gallbladder cancer found incidentally at cholecystectomy: 5-year survival is 40-60% for T1 tumours confined to mucosa/muscle, dropping to 10-20% for T2 tumours penetrating the serosa. Gallbladder cancer is more common in East Africa than Western populations (age-standardized rate 3.5/100,000 vs 1.5/100,000) — histology of every gallbladder specimen is mandatory.
prognosis.landmarkReference: Yokoe M, Hata J, Takada T, et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholecystitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):41-54.
